comments.text: Here's some further non-exhaustive context and speculation. Before the work of Davies and Illingworth, in this paper the same upper bounds were confirmed for the fractional chromatic number $\chi_f(G)$. And slightly worse bounds were given on the chromatic number $\chi(G)$, a factor $\sqrt{2}$ higher for the upper bound on $f(n)$, and a factor $2^{1/3}$ higher for the upper bound on $g(n)$. Moreover, it was shown that $\chi_f(G) \leq (\sqrt{2}+o(1)) \sqrt{\frac{n}{\log n}}$ and $\chi_f(G) \leq (18^{1/3}+o(1)) \frac{m^{1/3}}{(\log m)^{2/3}}$ hold for all $\textit{regular}$ triangle-free graphs $G$ on $n$ vertices and $m$ edges. This (and other evidence) motivated a conjecture that the condition of regularity could be dropped, which was later confirmed in a very nice paper of Martinsson and Steiner. Perhaps the same will be true for the chromatic number instead, thus improving the state of the art for Problem 1104. The vague intuition is that the largest independent set $\alpha(G)$ seems to govern the right answer; we know that $\chi(G)\geq \frac{n}{\alpha(G)}$, but for this type of problem it appears that also something like $\max_{|G|=n}\chi(G) \lessapprox \frac{n}{\min_{|G|=n}\alpha(G)}$ holds. Plugging in Shearer's lower bound $(1/\sqrt{2}+o(1)) \sqrt{n \log n}$ on the independence number leads to the guess that $\chi(G) \leq (\sqrt{2}+o(1)) \sqrt{\frac{n}{\log n}}$. Following the conjectures made in the latest breakthroughs papers on $R(3,t)$, one might even guess tight bounds $\alpha(G) \geq (1+o(1)) \sqrt{n \log n}$ and $\chi(G)\leq (1+o(1)) \sqrt{\frac{n}{\log n}}$. (And from there the heavy speculation continues; e.g. is the (list) chromatic number of a triangle-free graph with maximum degree $\Delta$ at most $(1/2+o(1)) \frac{\Delta}{\log \Delta} $ ? This would be tight. By the Johansson-Molloy theorem, it is currently known with $(1+o(1))$ instead, and this was used to prove some of the results mentioned above.)
